title: Forfar Bridie
subtitle: A Scottish hand pie of seasoned beef and onion folded into a half-moon pastry
excerpt: The Forfar bridie is a Scottish savoury pasty: seasoned beef mince and onion enclosed in a rough hot water crust pastry folded into a half-moon and crimped on top, not along the side as with a Cornish pasty. It is a lunch-box staple from Angus.
sourceNotes: Traditional Scottish baking from Forfar, Angus. F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929): bridie recipe with suet pastry. Traditional Scots hand-pie practice, Tayside region.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked bridies for up to 2 months. Bake from frozen at 190°C for 55-60 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The recipe doubles easily. Make a larger batch and freeze half.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assembled unbaked bridies can be refrigerated for up to 24 hours. Egg wash just before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Scottish savoury pastry from the town of Forfar in Angus, traditionally filled with beef and onion in a hot water or shortcrust pastry case, folded into a horseshoe or half-moon and crimped along the top edge rather than the side. Bridies are distinct from Cornish pasties in shape, pastry type, and filling.

The distinctive top crimp of a Forfar bridie, formed by pressing two layers of pastry together and folding and twisting the overlapping edge into a series of small flutes along the curved top of the half-moon shape.

content.text: differs from the Cornish pasty in one key respect: it is crimped along the top of the curved edge, not along the side. The filling is raw seasoned beef, onion, and nothing more; no potato, no carrot. This simplicity is the point.
The traditional pastry was made with suet; shortcrust is an acceptable modern substitute. The
takes practice but is not structurally necessary: a simple fork-pressed edge seals equally well.
and Worcestershire sauce if using. The filling must be raw when it goes into the pastry. Do not add stock or egg; the natural juices are sufficient.

into 4 equal pieces. Roll each into a rough oval about 22 cm long and 3-4 mm thick. Place a quarter of the filling on one half of each oval, leaving a 2 cm border. Moisten the border with water. Fold the empty half over the filling to form a half-moon and press the edges firmly together. Crimp with a fork along the curved top edge. Chill for 30 minutes on a lined baking tray.
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Brush the bridies with beaten egg. Make a small steam vent in the top of each. Bake for 40-45 minutes until deeply golden. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

rows.symptom: Juices leak from the seam during baking
rows.cause: Edge not sealed firmly enough, or too much filling used
rows.fix: Press and crimp firmly; moisten the edges before joining. Leave a 2 cm unfilled border.

rows.cause: Beef mince was too lean, or overbaked
rows.fix: Use mince with at least 20% fat. Pull the bridies at 40 minutes.
Check with an oven thermometer. Brush with undiluted beaten egg before baking.

rows.symptom: Pastry shrinks and uncovers the filling
rows.cause: Pastry was stretched rather than rolled to size
rows.fix: Roll to size without pulling; stretched pastry always springs back in the oven.

content.text: Forfar bridies come from the town of Forfar in Angus, Scotland, where they have been sold by bakers since at least the 1850s. They remain a staple in Scottish baker's shops and butchers, and are a regional counterpart to the English pasty tradition. Eaten hot from the oven or cold for a packed lunch, they need nothing alongside.
